coredata.dc:title: Chitosan capped Ag/NiS nanocomposites: A novel colorimetric probe for detection of L-cysteine at nanomolar level and its anti-microbial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, B. Hari
Okla, Mohammad K.
Abdel-maksoud, Mostafa A.
Al-Qahtani, Wahidah H.
AbdElgawad, Hamada
Altukhayfi, Munirah Saud
Thomas, Ajith M.
Raju, Lija L.
Khan, S. Sudheer
coredata.dc:description: L-Cysteine (L-cys) plays very crucial role in biological systems. The study reports the colorimetric detection of L-cys at nanomolar level using chitosan capped Ag decorated NiS nanocomposite (chit-Ag/NiS NCs).The chemical reduction and co-precipitation methods were adopted to prepare chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The fabricated NCs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), FT-Raman, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The chit-Ag/NiS NCs particularly detect L-cys even in other amino acids presence. The chit-Ag/NiS NCs showed the surface charge of −26 ± 39.9 mV. The detection of L-cys was indicated by disappearance of yellowish-brown color of Chit-Ag/NiS NCs to colorless. A good linear correlation was found between absorbance vs logarithmic concentration of L-cys (1 μM to 1 nM) with R2 value of 0.99. The chit-Ag/NiS NCs impregnated cotton swabs was prepared for real time detection of L-cys and the prepared probe was found to be highly selective and specific. The effect of pH, temperature and salinity influencing the L-cys detection was studied. Also, the antimicrobial activity of Chit-Ag/NiS NCs was investigated against gram negative (E. coli) and gram positive (B. subtilis) bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Ag/NiS
L-cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cotton swabs incorporated with chit-Ag/NiS NCs
Effect of temperature, pH and salinity
The antimicrobial effect of chit-Ag/NiS NCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/374) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saud Arabia. Authors sincerely thank DST-FIST facility at Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Tamil Nadu, India for helping us with TGA analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectra of Ag/NiS and NiS, (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of chit-Ag/NiS NCs and (c) FTIR spectrum of chit-Ag/NiS NCs, chit-Ag-NiS-Cys, Ag/NiS and NiS.
(a) TEM image of chit-Ag/NiS NCs and (b) TEM image with d-spacing values.

(a) SEM image of chit-Ag/NiS NCs before interaction with L-cys, (b) SEM image of chit-Ag/NiS NCs after interaction with L-cys, (c) elemental mapping of chit-Ag/NiS NCs and (d) EDAX of chit-Ag/NiS NCs.
XPS survey spectrum of (a) chit-Ag/NiS NCs, (b) Ag 3d, (c) N 1s, (d) Ni 2p, (e) O 1s and (f) S 2p.
(a) TGA graph of chit-Ag/NiS NCs and (b) FT-Raman spectra of before and after interaction of chit-Ag/NiS NCs with L-cys.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of different amino acids interacted with chit-Ag/NiS NCs with inset image and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of different volume of L-cys with inset image of the vials interacted with different volume of L-cys.
(a) Different concentration of L-cys with inset calibration curve and (b) interference studies to understand the effect of detection of L-cys.
Optimization studies (a) pH, (b) temperature, and (c) salinity.
Cotton swab based probe for selective detection of cysteine.
Antimicrobial activity of chit-Ag/NiS NCs. Zone of inhibition of (a) E. coli and (b) B. subtilis at different volume of NCs.
Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism towards L-cys detection using the prepared probe.
Cytotoxicity of Ag/NiS NCs, chit Ag/NiS NCs and chit-Ag/NiS + L-cys.
Comparison of analytical methods used for determining L-cys.

head.ce:title: Chitosan capped Ag/NiS nanocomposites: A novel colorimetric probe for detection of L-cysteine at nanomolar level and its anti-microbial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: L-Cysteine (L-cys) plays very crucial role in biological systems. The study reports the colorimetric detection of L-cys at nanomolar level using chitosan capped Ag decorated NiS nanocomposite (chit-Ag/NiS NCs).The chemical reduction and co-precipitation methods were adopted to prepare chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The fabricated NCs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), FT-Raman, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The chit-Ag/NiS NCs particularly detect L-cys even in other amino acids presence. The chit-Ag/NiS NCs showed the surface charge of −26 ± 39.9 mV. The detection of L-cys was indicated by disappearance of yellowish-brown color of Chit-Ag/NiS NCs to colorless. A good linear correlation was found between absorbance vs logarithmic concentration of L-cys (1 μM to 1 nM) with R2 value of 0.99. The chit-Ag/NiS NCs impregnated cotton swabs was prepared for real time detection of L-cys and the prepared probe was found to be highly selective and specific. The effect of pH, temperature and salinity influencing the L-cys detection was studied. Also, the antimicrobial activity of Chit-Ag/NiS NCs was investigated against gram negative (E. coli) and gram positive (B. subtilis) bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: L-Cysteine (L-cys) is an important amino acid which is present in most of the proteins [1]. L-cys contains thiol groups where it is used in the field of medicine, cosmetic and food industries [1]. In biologics, L-cys plays a crucial role in protein's secondary structure, and the disulphide bond present in the quaternary and tertiary structure of protein [2]. L-cys binds strongly to metal ions which balances the intercellular concentration and hence protecting the cells from damages caused by high heavy metal ions concentration [3]. L-cys is involved in oxidation and reduction of several acylation and alkylation processes [3]. Among the protein present in the human genome, it encodes about 214,000 of L-cys which is widely distributed in the body [4]. L-cys can be incorporated through different pathways depending on the need of the cells in human [5]. In human metabolic system, L-cys act as a source of sulphide content, in presence or absence causes various human health problems such as coronary artery and brain disorder that leads to nerve cell damage and cause Parkinson disease [6]. L-cys is widely used in various drug related applications [7]. The abnormal concentration of L-cys leads to hyper homocysteinemia which leads to various cardiovascular diseases and liver defects [8]. Nevertheless, L-cys in insufficient amount can cause a decline in hematopoietic function and leucopenia [9,10]. Hence, the selective and sensitive sensing of L-cys concentration is of great significance in analytical technique in recent years. According to the literature, there are lot of analytical testing for detecting L-cys including capillary electrophoresis, mass spectroscopy, and voltammetry [11]. However, most of the techniques are expensive and complicated method for on-site sensing of L-cys.

The above problems lead to the development of chitosan capped Ag decorated NiS nanocomposite (chit-Ag/NiS NCs) for sensitive and specific recognition of L-cys. Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) plays significant role in the field related to bio-medical applications such as antibacterial, anti-angiogenesis, anti-platelet, anti-inflammatory, antifungal, and anti-viral properties owing to its surface plasma resonance (SPR) [12,13]. Ag NPs are widely used in the field of biosensor, medical diagnosis, catalysis, nanoelectronics, and drug delivery [14,15]. Different methods used to synthesis Ag NPs are chemical and physical methods [16,17].

Ag NPs possesses strong antibacterial properties against variety of bacterial species such as the gram positive and gram-negative bacteria [18]. The bacterial growth can be inactivated by interaction of Ag NPs with the thiol groups by penetrating the bacterial cell membranes which results in cell death [19]. In this study, Chit-Ag/NiS NCs was developed for sensing L-cys at nano molar level and the multifunctional efficiency was tested by performing anti-microbial activity against B. subtilis and E. coli. The cotton probe-based sensor kit developed is proved to be effective for onsite monitoring of L-cys. Chit-Ag/NiS NCs showed lower detection limit and higher selectivity making it as a promising candidate to implement in environmental analysis, pharmaceutical analysis, and clinical diagnosis.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), nickel Chloride (NiCl2), sodium sulphide flakes (Na2S), and various amino acids include tyrosine, methionine, arginine, glycine, alanine, proline, phenylalanine, glutamine, L-cys, homocysteine and glutathione were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory Private Limited, India. All chemicals are analytical grade and used without further purification.

The NiS NPs were synthesized by chemical co-precipitation method. Here, 0.5 M of nickel chloride (50 mL) and 0.1 M of sodium sulphide (50 mL) were prepared separately. Then, sodium sulphide solution was added drop by drop into nickel chloride solution under stirring for 2 h at 70 °C, and the mixture was centrifuged at 7826 ×g for 10 min. Then, the collected precipitate was washed two times with DI water and ethanol respectively. The particles were lyophilized and stored for further use.

The chit-Ag/NiS NCs were prepared by sonicating 5 mg of NiS NPs in 50 mL of distilled water. AgNO3 solution (0.1 M) of 100 μL was added to the above sonicated NiS NPs (20 mL) solution and mixed well. Thereafter, 30 mL of ice-cold sodium borohydride (0.1 M) and 3 mL of 1% chitosan were mixed together. To the mixture, AgNO3 solution (20 mL) was added drop by drop under dark condition. The yellowish-brown color appearance indicates the formation of chit-Ag/NiS NCs.

The nanomaterial's optical property, structural property and its morphology were characterized by using UV–visible spectrophotometer, TEM, SEM, EDAX, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, Raman spectroscopy, UV–vis DRS, TGA, and particle size analyzer. The zetapotential was determined using zetasizer (Malvern, UK).

The colorimetric sensing of L-cys was performed by change in color variation by the interaction of chit-Ag/NiS NCs with L-cys in the ratio of 3:2. In order to observe the selectivity of the synthesized probe, the chit-Ag/NiS NCs was interacted with various amino acids including tyrosine, methionine, arginine, tyrosine, glycine, alanine, proline, phenylalanine, glutamine, glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcy).The different concentration of L-cys from 1 mM to 1 nM was tested to understand the sensitivity. The different volume of L-cys was tested from (100 μL to 1 nM) for optimizing the ratio between NCs and the amino acid. The interference study was performed to understand the detection efficiency of the probe in presence of other amino acids interfering in L-cys detection. For the above tests, the UV–vis spectra were recorded from 200 to 600 nm. A control experiment was run only with mixture of amino acids in the absence of L-cys. Also, the influence of environment condition was performed at different temperature, pH and salinity towards L-cys detection for optimizing the environmental parameters which favors the L-cys detection. The effect of temperature on L-cys detection was investigated in the range from 10 to 60 °C at pH 7. The effect of pH was investigated from pH 5.0 to 9.0 at 37 °C. The different saline concentration from 0.1 to 0.8% was performed to understand the influence of NaCl concentration on detection of L-cys. The entire experiment was performed in triplicates to minimize the errors in the performed experiments.

The tap water was collected from Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, India. The small particles and dust particles were removed by using Whattman filter paper. Moreover, accuracy and other amino acid interference were understood by the quantitative detection of L-cys. Here, the known concentration of L-cys with various amino acids was spiked into the samples and the experiment was performed to detect L-cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cotton swabs incorporated with chit-Ag/NiS NCs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare a portable sensor kit, we developed a user-friendly chit-Ag/NiS NCs impregnated on cotton swabs. Typically, the cotton swabs were dipped in the chit-Ag/NiS NCs solution and thereafter the cotton was air dried. The prepared cotton swabs were spiked with different amino acids. The detection was observed by change in color of L-cys spiked cotton swab where the apparent change was noticed and recorded.

The antimicrobial activity of NCs was performed by well diffusion method. The bacterial strains of E. coli (gram negative) and B. subtilis (gram positive) were used for the study. The bacterial strain were taken freshly and swabbed on the nutrient agar plate uniformly using sterile cotton swab and the well was made on the agar plates by using gel puncture. Then, different volume of chit-Ag/NiS NCs (50, 100 and 150 μL) were added to the respective wells. The agar dish was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and the anti-microbial activity was evaluated by measuring the zone of inhibition.

The XRD pattern of NiS and chit-Ag/NiS NCs are depicted in Fig. 1a. XRD pattern reveals the presence of Ag where the characteristic peaks at 38.05°, 44.26°, 64.42° and 77.45° are assigned to the planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311) [20]. While the peaks located at 20°, 30°, 38.3°, 44° and 64° corresponds to (100), (101), (110), (103) and (200) planes belonging to NiS respectively [21]. The XRD peaks confirm the decoration of Ag NPs on the NiS surface.

The synthesized chit-Ag/NiS NCs were characterized preliminarily by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Chit-Ag/NiS NCs shows λmax at 402 nm due to SPR effect (Fig. 1b). The NCs possessed the zeta potential of −26 ± 39.9 mV and the fabricated chit-Ag/NiS NCs exhibited higher stability even after 1 month.

FT-IR spectra of chit-Ag/NiS NCs, Ag/NiS, NiS and chit-Ag/NiS interacted L-cys are shown in Fig. 1c. In the FT-IR spectrum of NiS, the bands at 637, 650 and 950 cm−1 were related to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretch of the NiS bond in NiS NPs [22]. The peaks at 637, 650 and 950 cm−1are belonging to the NiS stretching vibration mode, where the peak at 1793, 2183 cm−1 is owing to the doped Ag. In presence of chitosan, the spectrum shows the peak at 1609 cm−1 belonging to CC weak stretching and quivering at region from 1376 to 1025 cm−1 correspond to NH bond of primary or secondary amides. The peak at 775 cm−1 was assigned to covalent OH [23]. Further, the peaks near the region at 3334 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibrations of OH groups [24]. The vibration peak near the region 1390 cm−1 represents the CH3 vibrational mode, and the one at 1274 cm−1 belongs to CO carboxylic acids bond stretching vibration. These results confirm that Ag/NiS NPs are successfully functionalized by chitosan.

The morphology of chit-Ag/NiS NCs was observed using HR-TEM analysis and it is depicted in Fig. 2a. The TEM image showed the successful fabrication of chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The dispersion of Ag/NiS NCs on chitosan matrix can be observed clearly in Fig. 2a inset where it shows the wrinkled shaped Ag/NiS embedded on the surface of chitosan. Fig. 2b depicts the d-spacing of 0.11 and 0.12 nm belongs to the (111) facets of NiS and (311) facets of Ag NPs respectively. These results confirm the successful formation of chit-Ag/NiS NCs.

The SEM image for Ag/NiS NCs is shown in Fig. 3a. The SEM image confirms that the Ag/NiS NCs are entrapped on chitosan matrix. After the chit-Ag/NiS NCs was conjugated with L-cys, the size and morphology of the NCs was obviously increased, and the surface becomes smooth in some extent (Fig. 3b). The significant change in morphology and size indicated the successful conjugation of chit-Ag/NiS NCs with L-cys. The dispersion of elements on chitosan matrix was studied by SEM-EDAX surface mapping analyses. As shown in Fig. 3c, mapping analysis reveals that Ni and Ag were the elements present in the prepared NCs. These elements were rapidly distributed in the matrix and the color of Ni in the elemental map become clear. The Ni and Ag peak were evidenced in EDAX spectrum of chit-Ag/NiS NCs (Fig. 3d). The results confirmed the successful formation of chit-Ag/NiS NCs.

The chemical states and the elemental composition were investigated using XPS spectra. The elements such as Ni, S, O, N, C and Ag are confirmed by the XPS spectra depicted in Fig. 4a. Fig. 4b represents the XPS spectrum of Ag 3d where the two peaks at 372.5 and 366.57 eV corresponds to Ag 3d1/2 and Ag 3d3/2 respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectra exhibited two peaks of different nitrogen atoms, where 399.8 eV assigned to –NH2 group and 398.6 eV assigned to CN group (Fig. 4c). The Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 4d) was deconvoluted into two peaks centred at 855.2 and 873.9 eV attributed to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 whereas the spin-orbit splitting was estimated to be 18.7 eV. The O1s peaks at 530.1 eV correspond to the lattice oxygen (Fig. 4e). The S 2p peaks at 157 and 163 eV belongs to S 2p5/2 and S 2p3/2 (Fig. 4f).

Fig. 5a shows the TGA curves of chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The three weight losses are observed in the TGA curve. The overall weight loss exhibited was around 20%. The first weight loss of 3% took place in the temperature range between 38 and 110 °C as seen in the derivative curve due to the dehydration of the sample. The main chain of chitosan was destructed after 150 °C leading to weight loss of 5%. The major process involved is the release of H2O, CH3COOH, NH3, CO2 and CO that were observed in the range of temperature from 300 °C causing a weight loss of 12%. There is a weight loss of 20% at 100 °C. The degradation was slightly similar with previously reported literatures for chitosan degradation [25,26].

Fig. 6a depicts the UV–vis absorption spectra of various amino acids such as tyrosine, methionine, arginine, tryptophan, glycine, alanine, proline, phenylalanine, glutamine, GSH and Hcy interacted with chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The results reveal that the peak intensity of L-cys alone disappeared completely after interaction with chit-Ag/NiS NCs. The inset image shows the vials containing the various amino acids interacted with NCs and the color disappearance was noticed only in the vial added with L-cys from yellow to colorless. In addition, the high surface area to volume ratio of chit-Ag/NiS NCs made it efficient for surface interaction with L-cys that causes the shift in LSPR absorption band. The peak shift can also depend on structure and shape of the NPs.

Different volume of L-cys interaction with NCs was also investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectra for different volumes of L-cys in the range from 100 μL to 1000 μL interacted with NCs is depicted in Fig. 6b. The increase in volume of L-cys leads to the peak intensity decrease. The inset image shows the NCs interacted with different volumes of L-cys (Fig. 6b).

The UV–visible absorption spectra of various concentrations of L-cys from 1 mM to 1 nM after interaction with chit-Ag/NiS NCs are shown in Fig. 7a. The result shows that chit-Ag/NiS NCs peak decreased with increase in L-cys concentrations. The inset image (Fig. 7a) shows the linear relationship (y = 0.3327x − 0.8875, R2 = 0.992) between L-cys concentration and the absorbance at 402 nm. A good linear relationship was found with R2 value of 0.992. The detection limit of the prepared probe was 6.5 nM where the LOD was determined and calculated using the following equation [27]:
(1)
Limit of detection =
3
σ
slope
where sigma stands for standard deviation (SD).

The determination of L-cys concentration in aqueous medium was carried by using the standard graph. Here, L-cys of different concentrations from known standard solution (30 nM and 55 nM) was spiked to the water and the probe was used to detect the same. The probe was selectively detected L-cys with a recovery of 99.9%. The relative standard deviations of six measurements were taken and it was found to be less than 1%.

Fig. 7b depicts the results of interference study. There was no shift in the peak on addition of other amino acids expect in absence of L-cys. The peak intensity was disappeared completely when other amino acids interacted with NCs in presence of L-cys. It is evident from the interference study that the developed chit-Ag/NiS NCs can be used effectively for the sensing of L-cys selectively even in presence of other amino acids. Few studies were reported the effective detection of L-cys by fluorescent probe. Sun et al. [28] reported the fluorescent probe using Naph-EA-mal for the selective detection of L-cys, GSH and Hcy which contains thiol group. Few studies were reported the effective detection of L-cys by fluorescent probe [29–35]. The present study focused to detect L-cys by colorimetric method. Table 1 shows the comparison of the sensors reported previously for L-cys sensing. On comparing with the other sensors, the developed nano sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards L-cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature, pH and salinity
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature, pH and saline concentration on detection of L-cys was performed to optimize the environmental parameters. The effect of pH in the aqueous medium is an important factor which affects the detection of L-cys. The influence of pH was studied in the range of pH 5.0 to 9.0. The effective detection was observed at pH 9.0 as shown in Fig. 8a. The result shows that significant change is appeared at higher pH range.

The impact of temperature on L-cys detection was investigated. Fig. 8b shows the effect of temperature from 10 to 60 °C on L-cys detection and it was found that there is no prominent change in detection of L-cys on varying the temperature. The effect of salinity has been performed at different percentage of NaCl from 0.1 to 0.8% and it is depicted in Fig. 8c. The study showed no variation in detection efficiency at all the saline concentrations tested.

The NCs impregnated cotton swabs were made to interact with different amino acids. From Fig. 9 , it is clear that L-cys is interacted selectively and the prepared cotton swab was very specific to L-cys. The stability of the probe was studied for one month and it did not show any variation in results. Hence, the developed cotton swab probe with chit-Ag NiS/NCs can be used to detect L-cys from real time sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antimicrobial effect of chit-Ag/NiS NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial activity of chit-Ag/NiS NCs was investigated through well diffusion method. The antimicrobial activity was performed against E. coli and B. subtilis. When comparing E. coli (gram negative) (Fig. 10a) and B. subtilis (gram positive), the zone of inhibition was higher in E. coli (gram negative). The zone of inhibition at different volume of NCs such as 50 μL, 100 μL and 150 μL was 18, 22 and 25 mm for E. coli and 14, 17, and 18 mm for B. subtilis respectively. Higher zone of inhibition was observed in E. coli than B. subtilis. The gram-negative bacteria possess thin peptidoglycan (PG) layer where the NCs can penetrate inside the cell easily and it repairs the DNA when compared with B. subtilis possessing thick peptidoglycan layer [37,38]. The structure of the NPs plays an important role where it can penetrate the cell wall of bacteria effectively.

The systematic mechanism that can be possible for detection of L-cys using chit-Ag/NiS NCs is represented in Fig. 11 . The complex formation between L-cys and Ag/NiS NCs is due to the interaction of sulfhydryl group (thiol) in L-cys with the sulphur group in NiS NPs [39]. It has high affinity to the thiol groups present in amino acids. The thiol group in L-cys such as metallothionine and sulphide groups will bind with NiS NPs, through chitosan which is a linear polysaccharide group [25]. The chitosan acts as a matrix for the decoration of Ag on the surface of NiS. The thiol group in L-cys carries a negative charge (sulphide ion), which interacts with the positively charged metal ions [40]. This conjugation is due to the formation of AgS covalent bond between chit-Ag/NiS NCs and thiol group present in L-cys. This sensing is further studied by FT-Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 5b depicts the Raman spectra of chit-Ag/NiS NCs where the peaks in the region from 200 to 400 cm−1 denotes the lattice vibration of atoms present in NiS. After the interaction with L-cys, the peaks of NiS disappeared completely. This change is due to interaction of the thiol group present in L-cys with the sulphur in NiS to form a complex [41]. Also, the interaction with Ag induces the decrease in SPR absorbance band. This phenomenon leads to the aggregation of chit-Ag/NiS NCs. In particle size analysis, the chit-Ag/NiS NCs were conjugated with L-cys where the size and morphology of the NCs was obviously increased, and the surface becomes smooth in some extent (Fig. 3b). The significant change in morphology and size indicated that the L-cys has been successfully conjugated with chit-Ag/NiS NCs. In XPS spectra, the peak of S 2p shifts negatively from 157 to 161 eV might be due to the increase of valence electron density up on interaction (Fig. 4e). These results confirms us the detailed mechanism of detection of L-cys.

The cytotoxicity study of the prepared chit-Ag/NiS NCs probe was conducted by MTT (3-[4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay with Hela cells. The cell density of 1 × 104 cell/well (100 μL) was injected into a 96-well cell culture dish and the incubation of the dishes was carried out in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. The Chit-Ag/NiS NCs, Ag/NiS NCs and Chit-Ag/NiS NCs + L-cys (2 mg/ml) were dispersed in DMEM which are injected into the wells (Fig. 12 ). Further, the plates were kept in a CO2 incubator with an incubation period of 24 h and then 5 mg/ml MTT solution (20 μL) was added to each of the wells. The plate was then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h where the charge of L-cys has decreased predominantly. This can increase the permeability of the semi-permeable membrane of chosen the cell. The results observed were similar to the studies of Celestina et al. [42] where they have evaluated the cytotoxicity of NPs in presence of Co2+. The results show that the presence of Co2+ reduced the cytotoxicity. In our study we found that NCs showed cytotoxicity against Hela cells and the presence of Cys reduced the cytotoxicity of NCs.

In conclusion, we have prepared chit-Ag/NiS NCs through a simple reduction method for the sensitive and selective sensing of L-cys. The synthesized chit-Ag/NiS NCs was characterized by UV–Vis spectrophotometer, FT-IR, XRD, EDAX, XPS, SEM, Raman and HR-TEM techniques. The chit-Ag/NiS NCs was used as a probe to sense L-cys which is based on SPR band absorbance changes and the detection limit of L-cys was calculated to be 6.5 nM. The synthesized probe can be used for effective quantification of L-cys at nanomolar level and it showed an excellent cell permeability, and less toxicity towards the detection of L-cys in living cells. We applied the prepared probe in different environmental conditions for the detection of L-cys. Importantly, the prepared cotton swab probe can be used as a colorimetric kit for onsite monitoring of L-cys. The particle also proved to be an excellent anti-bacterial agent.

The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/374) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saud Arabia. Authors sincerely thank DST-FIST facility at Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Tamil Nadu, India for helping us with TGA analysis.
